name: Speed, Distance and Time
whatIsIt: SPEED tells how fast — distance covered in each unit of time (like 60 km per hour). The three are linked: Speed = Distance ÷ Time; Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance ÷ Speed. Cover the one you want in the triangle D over S and T, and the formula appears.
whyNeeded: Journeys, sports, and everyday planning all use speed: 'when will we arrive?', 'how far in 2 hours?', 'what's my running pace?'. It's the most common real-life 'rate', and the gateway to physics.
realLifeUses: Working out arrival time for a bus or train journey
How far a car travels in 3 hours at 50 km/h

vocabulary.meaning: Distance covered per unit of time (km/h, m/s).
D on top, S and T below: cover what you want to find.

story.title: Will We Reach by Noon?
story.text: The family drives at a steady 60 km/h and the town is 180 km away. 'Will we reach by noon if we leave at 9?' asks Meera. She uses Time = Distance ÷ Speed = 180 ÷ 60 = 3 hours. 'Leaving at 9, we arrive at 12 — exactly noon!' Papa is impressed with her planning.
story.extractedProblem: At 60 km/h, how long to travel 180 km?

visual.caption: Each hour covers 60 km. Three 60s make 180 km → the trip takes 3 hours (Time = Distance ÷ Speed).

teachingGallery.title: The three formulas
teachingGallery.note: Cover what you want in the D / S · T triangle.

examples.caption: Steady speed adds the same distance each hour.

standardMethod.summary: Pick the right formula from the D-S-T triangle, keep units matching, then calculate.
steps: Write what you know and what you want (distance, speed, or time).
Speed = Distance ÷ Time; Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance ÷ Speed.
Keep units consistent (km with hours → km/h; m with seconds → m/s).
Check the answer is sensible (faster speed → less time for the same distance).

workedExamples.problem: A car travels at 50 km/h for 3 hours. How far does it go?
A cyclist covers 30 km in 2 hours. What is the speed?

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying when you should divide (using Speed × Time to find time).
commonMistakes.fix: Use the triangle: to find TIME, cover T → Distance ÷ Speed. To find DISTANCE, cover D → Speed × Time.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing units, like minutes with km/h, without converting.
commonMistakes.fix: Match the units first: with km/h use HOURS; 30 minutes = 0.5 hour.

easy.q: At 40 km/h for 2 hours, how far (in km)?
60 km in 1 hour is a speed of how many km/h?

medium.q: At 60 km/h, how long to travel 120 km (in hours)?
A train covers 240 km in 4 hours. Speed (km/h)?

challenge.q: At 80 km/h for 2.5 hours, how far (in km)?
To find the TIME for a journey you should…

questions.q: 100 km at 25 km/h takes how many hours?

teachBackPrompt: Plan a real trip with a grown-up: pick a distance and a speed, and work out the time (or pick a time and speed and work out the distance). Explain how the D-S-T triangle tells you whether to multiply or divide.

revisionCard.summary: Speed = Distance ÷ Time; Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance ÷ Speed. Use the D-over-S·T triangle: cover what you want. Keep units matched (km/h with hours; 30 min = 0.5 h). Faster speed → less time for the same distance.

rememberIt.hook: The D-S-T triangle: cover the one you want
rememberIt.unpack: D on top, S and T below. Distance = S x T. Speed = D/T. Time = D/S. Units must match (km with hours).

studentQuestions.q: How do I remember the three formulas?
studentQuestions.a: Draw a triangle: D on top, S and T below. Cover the one you want - D = S x T, S = D/T, T = D/S.

studentQuestions.q: Do the units have to match?
studentQuestions.a: Yes - km with hours gives km/h; mixing km and minutes gives nonsense. Convert first.
